title: Epic dependencies with directed edges and cycle detection

story.i_want_to: create and remove directed dependency edges between epics (epic A depends_on epic B), list all epic dependencies for a project, and have the system reject dependency additions that would create cycles
story.so_that: the orchestrator can determine which epics must complete before others can begin, and the dependency graph remains a valid DAG (directed acyclic graph)

rationale: Epic dependencies define the sequencing constraints for project work. Without cycle detection, a circular dependency (A depends on B depends on C depends on A) would create an unsatisfiable constraint that blocks all dependent epics forever. The directed edge model (A depends_on B means B must complete before A) is simple, explicit, and enables topological sorting for scheduling.

acceptance_criteria.description: A Loopctl.WorkBreakdown.EpicDependency schema exists with fields: id, tenant_id, epic_id (the dependent), depends_on_epic_id (the prerequisite), inserted_at
POST /api/v1/epic_dependencies (role: user) creates a dependency edge; requires epic_id and depends_on_epic_id
Creating a dependency that would form a cycle returns 422 with error message 'Cycle detected in dependency graph'
Creating a self-dependency (epic_id == depends_on_epic_id) returns 422 with error message
Creating a duplicate dependency (same epic_id + depends_on_epic_id) returns 409 Conflict
Both epics must belong to the same project; cross-project dependencies are rejected with 422
DELETE /api/v1/epic_dependencies/:id (role: user) removes a dependency edge
GET /api/v1/projects/:id/epic_dependencies (role: agent+) returns all epic dependency edges for a project
All operations are scoped to the current tenant via RLS and application-level checks
RLS policy is enabled on the epic_dependencies table enforcing tenant isolation
Every mutation (create, update, delete) MUST be recorded in the audit log via Ecto.Multi in the same transaction as the entity change.

Epic dependency creation MUST validate cross-level consistency: if Epic A depends on Epic B, then no story in Epic B may depend (directly or transitively via story_dependencies) on any story in Epic A. Creating an epic dependency that would cause such a cross-level deadlock MUST return 422 with a clear error message identifying the conflicting story dependencies.

test_cases.name: Create valid epic dependency
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id, number: 1}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id, number: 2}) as epic_b
expected_results: Response status is 201
Response body has the dependency edge with both epic IDs
Dependency exists in database with correct tenant_id

test_cases.name: Reject direct cycle (A depends on B, B depends on A)
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_b.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_a.id})
expected_results: Response status is 422
Error message contains 'Cycle detected in dependency graph'

test_cases.name: Reject transitive cycle (A->B->C->A)
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_c
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_b.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_a.id})
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_c.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_b.id})
expected_results: Response status is 422
Error message contains 'Cycle detected in dependency graph'

test_cases.name: Reject self-dependency
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
expected_results: Response status is 422
Error message indicates self-dependency is not allowed

test_cases.name: Reject cross-project epic dependency
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project_a.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project_b.id}) as epic_b
expected_results: Response status is 422
Error message indicates epics must belong to the same project

test_cases.name: Delete epic dependency
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_b.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_a.id}) as dep
expected_results: Response status is 200 or 204
Dependency no longer exists in database

test_cases.name: List epic dependencies for project
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_b.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_a.id})
expected_results: Response status is 200
Response contains the dependency edge with epic_id and depends_on_epic_id

test_cases.name: Mutation creates audit log entries for epic dependency operations
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b
steps: POST /api/v1/epic_dependencies with body {"epic_id": "<epic_b.id>", "depends_on_epic_id": "<epic_a.id>"} and user API key
expected_results: Audit log entry created with entity_type='epic_dependency', entity_id=new_dep.id, action='created', actor_type='api_key', actor_id=api_key.id
Audit log entry created with entity_type='epic_dependency', entity_id=dep.id, action='deleted', actor_type='api_key', actor_id=api_key.id
All audit entries are in the same transaction as the entity changes (no orphaned entity without audit)

test_cases.name: Cross-level deadlock: epic dependency conflicts with story dependency
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_a.id, number: "1.1"}) as story_s1
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_b.id, number: "2.1"}) as story_s2
fixture(:story_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, story_id: story_s2.id, depends_on_story_id: story_s1.id})
expected_results: Response status is 422
Error message identifies the cross-level deadlock: Epic A depends on Epic B but story S2 (in Epic B) depends on story S1 (in Epic A)

test_cases.name: Cross-tenant isolation: tenant A cannot access tenant B data
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant_a.id, project_id: project_a.id}) as epic_a
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant_a.id, project_id: project_a.id}) as epic_b
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant_a.id, epic_id: epic_b.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_a.id})
expected_results: Returns empty list or 404, never tenant_a's data

technical_notes: Cycle detection algorithm: BFS/DFS from the proposed depends_on_epic_id following existing edges to see if we can reach epic_id. If yes, adding the edge would create a cycle.
Composite unique index: create unique_index(:epic_dependencies, [:epic_id, :depends_on_epic_id]) to prevent duplicates
Foreign keys: epic_id and depends_on_epic_id both reference epics(id)
The cycle detection must load all existing edges for the project and check the new edge against them — this is O(V+E) which is fine for expected graph sizes (<100 epics)
Consider implementing cycle detection in a dedicated Loopctl.WorkBreakdown.Graph module that can be reused for story dependencies
When an epic is deleted (US-6.1), cascade delete its dependency edges too
Schema module MUST use `use Loopctl.Schema` (provides `@primary_key {:id, :binary_id, autogenerate: true}`, `@foreign_key_type :binary_id`, `@timestamps_opts [type: :utc_datetime_usec]`)

Testing infrastructure: (1) All tests use async: true via DataCase/ConnCase, (2) Mox mocks defined in test/support/mocks.ex, (3) Default permissive stubs set in DataCase/ConnCase setup via stub_all_defaults/0 and Mox.set_mox_from_context(tags) for async isolation, (4) setup :verify_on_exit! in every test module, (5) Fixtures in test/support/fixtures.ex with build/2 (no DB) and fixture/2 (DB insert with auto-dependency resolution), (6) NEVER Application.put_env in tests — all service swapping via config/test.exs.
Config-based DI: behaviours resolved via Application.get_env(:loopctl, :key, DefaultModule). config/test.exs maps behaviours to Mox mocks. Tests use expect/3 to set specific expectations.
